raw_response:
```json
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement performs a kind of reflective self-disclosure, announcing an ongoing interior practice of metacognitive discernment. It signals to the reader (or to the self) that the speaker is cultivating a distinction rather than having already mastered it — framing the mind as a site of discipline and observation rather than passive reception.",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "There is a meaningful difference between thoughts that arise spontaneously and thoughts one actively endorses.",
    "Thoughts can occur to a person without that person being fully identified with or committed to them.",
    "This distinction is something that requires practice — it is not automatic or innate.",
    "The self or 'I' is separable from the stream of thought it observes.",
    "Endorsement implies some degree of agency, evaluation, or will applied to mental content.",
    "The practice itself has value — i.e., making this distinction is worth cultivating.",
    "There is a prior state in which this distinction was not being made, or was made poorly."
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent. The two poles of the distinction — occurrence versus endorsement — are intelligible and non-contradictory. The use of 'practicing' appropriately hedges the claim, avoiding any assertion of mastery while still asserting intentional effort. The statement draws on a well-established philosophical and psychological tradition (e.g., cognitive defusion in ACT therapy, Stoic distinctions between impressions and assent) without requiring that tradition to be named.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The reader or listener has experienced unwanted or intrusive thoughts and would benefit from knowing they need not identify with them.",
    "The speaker is genuinely engaged in reflective or therapeutic work where this distinction has practical consequences.",
    "The audience understands that 'endorsement' implies evaluative agency, not mere suppression.",
    "The distinction is being used to increase psychological flexibility rather than to avoid accountability for thought patterns.",
    "The poetic framing invites the reader into their own version of the practice rather than closing down interpretation."
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "The distinction is used to disclaim responsibility for harmful thoughts that do in fact reflect deep values or patterns the speaker has not examined.",
    "The framing implies a cleaner separation between occurrence and endorsement than is psychologically realistic, suggesting a level of self-transparency that may not be achievable.",
    "The statement is deployed performatively — to appear reflective or evolved — rather than as a genuine description of an ongoing practice.",
    "The listener interprets 'a thought I endorse' as requiring a formal, deliberate act of approval, thereby setting an unrealistically high bar for ownership of one's own cognition.",
    "The practice is mistaken for a complete ethical framework, allowing the speaker to treat all uncomfortable moral intuitions as 'mere occurrences' to be discarded rather than examined."
  ]
}
```

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement performs a kind of reflective self-disclosure, announcing an ongoing interior practice of metacognitive discernment. It signals to the reader (or to the self) that the speaker is cultivating a distinction rather than having already mastered it — framing the mind as a site of discipline and observation rather than passive reception.
implicit_claims: There is a meaningful difference between thoughts that arise spontaneously and thoughts one actively endorses.
Thoughts can occur to a person without that person being fully identified with or committed to them.
This distinction is something that requires practice — it is not automatic or innate.
The self or 'I' is separable from the stream of thought it observes.
Endorsement implies some degree of agency, evaluation, or will applied to mental content.
The practice itself has value — i.e., making this distinction is worth cultivating.
There is a prior state in which this distinction was not being made, or was made poorly.
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent. The two poles of the distinction — occurrence versus endorsement — are intelligible and non-contradictory. The use of 'practicing' appropriately hedges the claim, avoiding any assertion of mastery while still asserting intentional effort. The statement draws on a well-established philosophical and psychological tradition (e.g., cognitive defusion in ACT therapy, Stoic distinctions between impressions and assent) without requiring that tradition to be named.
conditions_for_usefulness: The reader or listener has experienced unwanted or intrusive thoughts and would benefit from knowing they need not identify with them.
The speaker is genuinely engaged in reflective or therapeutic work where this distinction has practical consequences.
The audience understands that 'endorsement' implies evaluative agency, not mere suppression.
The distinction is being used to increase psychological flexibility rather than to avoid accountability for thought patterns.
The poetic framing invites the reader into their own version of the practice rather than closing down interpretation.
conditions_for_misleading: The distinction is used to disclaim responsibility for harmful thoughts that do in fact reflect deep values or patterns the speaker has not examined.
The framing implies a cleaner separation between occurrence and endorsement than is psychologically realistic, suggesting a level of self-transparency that may not be achievable.
The statement is deployed performatively — to appear reflective or evolved — rather than as a genuine description of an ongoing practice.
The listener interprets 'a thought I endorse' as requiring a formal, deliberate act of approval, thereby setting an unrealistically high bar for ownership of one's own cognition.
The practice is mistaken for a complete ethical framework, allowing the speaker to treat all uncomfortable moral intuitions as 'mere occurrences' to be discarded rather than examined.
